What is TheraEpiFlow?

TheraEpiFlow is a comprehensive bioinformatics server designed for analyzing and predicting therapeutic epitopes. The platform integrates multiple computational tools to facilitate the identification of potential vaccine candidates and immunotherapeutic targets. Our server combines state-of-the-art algorithms for epitope prediction, immunogenicity analysis, and population coverage assessment.

Access our server by the GitHub

Access TheraEpiFlow on GitHub

Tutorial

Step 1: Preparing the environment to open the tool

1 - In Windows, click the “Search” bar at the bottom and search for Windows PowerShell; 2 - Type the command wsl --install to install the Windows Subsystem for Linux (WSL) and press Enter. This command will enable the features needed to run WSL and install the Ubuntu Linux distribution;

3 - Go back to the Search bar and search for WSL. Then replace “usurio” with the name you want to use as your username and press Enter. Next, enter a password and press Enter. Repeat the password and press Enter. It is normal for the password not to appear on the black screen as you type; 4 - When asked if you want to install the metrics collection, confirm by typing the letter “y” and then press Enter;
5 - Install Git by typing the command “sudo apt install git” and press Enter; 6 - Enter the password and press Enter; 7 - In your browser, access the link to the TheraEpiFlow tool folder available on GitHub (https://github.com/Davirbe/TheraEpiFlow);
8 - Return to the command prompt and enter the command “git clone” followed by the GitHub link where our TheraEpiFlow tool is located to clone the GitHub information to your computer. Then press Enter; 9 - Once GitHub is cloned, if you want to check if it’s already in your WSL, type the command “ls” and press Enter. If the cloning was successful, TheraEpiFlow will appear.
🖼️ Tutorial Image

Step 2: Accessing the tool

1 - To open the tool, type the command `cd TheraEpiFlow/` and press Enter. Next, to install the tool’s dependencies, type the command `bash setup.sh` and press Enter; 2 - You will be asked a few questions to confirm your acceptance of the terms of service. To accept, type “a” and press Enter;

3 - After installing miniconda and conda, you must activate conda. Type the command “conda activate TheraEpiFlow” and press Enter. If you cannot find conda, it is not yet available on your terminal. Close the WSL command prompt and reopen it. Then type the command “cd TheraEpiFlow/” and press Enter. Next, type the command “conda activate TheraEpiFlow”;
4 - To open the tool’s menu, type the command “python main.py” and press Enter;
5 - This will open the tool’s main menu with all available commands. It is very important that you read all the information in the menu and for each step of the tool to understand what is being done. Using the tool is quite simple; generally, just enter the requested information and press Enter.
🖼️ Tutorial Image

Step 3: Creating the project

1 - To launch TheraEpiFlow, press Enter. The first step is to create a new project (after your first use, the menu will display a list of the projects you have created), so type “n” and press Enter; 2 - Name your project (do not include spaces between words and avoid accents) and press Enter. Next, if you wish, add a note and press Enter. If you do not want to add notes, simply press Enter to proceed to the next step;

3 - Specify how many organisms you want to work with. In this example, I entered only 1 organism; 4 - Enter the full name of the organism, press Enter, then type “y” to confirm the name and press Enter;
5 - Enter a short name for the protein you are working with and press Enter. If you want the default, just press Enter. Then enter the number of proteins (in this example, I used the default = 1); 6 - Type the name of the protein for which you want to predict epitopes and press Enter. Then type “y” to confirm the protein name and press Enter. Next, if you wish, type the abbreviated name you want to give your protein (if not, just press Enter) and then press Enter;
7 - Select the source for the protein sequence (enter 1 for UniProt or 2 to use the FASTA sequence) and press Enter. Then type “y” and press Enter to confirm. As soon as the box with the information appears, type “y” and press Enter to confirm again.
🖼️ Tutorial Image
🖼️ Tutorial Image

Step 4: Defining the Protein Sequence

1 - In this step, the tool will search for protein sequences in the database specified in the previous step, and you will choose which sequence(s) you want to work with. To begin this step, press Enter. Information will then be provided about what happens during this search step and how the list of sequences is selected. Next, type “f” and press Enter. Then confirm the files that will be generated by the different prediction methods by typing “y” and pressing Enter;

2 - If you have the protein’s FASTA sequence, type “y,” press Enter, and enter the protein’s FASTA sequence. If you do not have it, type “n” and press Enter. When the sequence list appears, enter the number of the protein sequence(s) you want to work with;
3 - To view the information in the protein sequence .csv file, type 1 and press Enter. After reviewing the data, press Enter to proceed to the next step of epitope prediction.
🖼️ Tutorial Image
🖼️ Tutorial Image

Step 5: Epitope Prediction

1 - When the epitope prediction panel appears, press Enter to view information on how the prediction will be performed, then press Enter;

2 - If you want to perform the prediction for the panel of the 27 most common HLAs in the world, type “y”; if you want to predict specific HLAs, type “N”, press Enter, enter the HLAs, and press Enter;
3 - Enter the epitope length and press Enter;
4 - When the prediction is complete, enter option 1 and press Enter to view the first data from the .csv file containing the list of predicted epitopes. Then press Enter to proceed to the next consensus step.
🖼️ Tutorial Image
🖼️ Tutorial Image

Step 6: Consensus on Epitope Prediction

In this step, a table will be generated containing the epitopes that are common (consensus) to both prediction methods and are considered immunogenic (score > 0).

1 - Press Enter to review the methodology used in this step to generate the epitope consensus table;
2 - Press Enter to define the binding affinity based on percentile rank values (the lower the percentile, the higher the binding affinity). Then, enter the number corresponding to the desired percentile rank range and press Enter; 3 - Review the epitope values presented throughout the filtering steps for creating the consensus table. Then enter 1 and press Enter to view the first data from the consensus table in .csv format;
4 - Press Enter to proceed to the next step of identifying the toxicity potential of the epitopes.
🖼️ Tutorial Image

Step 7: Toxicity Screening

1 - Press Enter to view the methodology used in the step for identifying the toxicity potential of epitopes;

2 - Press Enter to set the toxicity threshold (recommended default threshold >= 0.35). Next, enter the number corresponding to the threshold and press Enter. To view the .csv table with the predicted epitopes and their toxicity data, enter 1 and press Enter;
3 - Press Enter to proceed to the next step of grouping the epitopes.
🖼️ Tutorial Image

Step 8: Cluster Analysis

1 - Press Enter to view the methodology explaining how epitopes with similar sequences are grouped;

2 - Next, press Enter to specify the percentage of sequence identity that epitopes must have to be considered part of the same group (e.g., 0.8 = 80%). Enter the value and press Enter; 3 - Enter the epitope grouping method and press Enter. We recommend using the cluster break method (method = 1);
4 - To view the complete grouping results table, enter 1 and press Enter;
5 - Press Enter to proceed to the next step.
🖼️ Tutorial Image

Step 9: Selection of Representative Epitopes for the Groups

In this step, the groups consisting of multiple epitopes formed in the previous step will undergo a normalization process based on HLA data and percentile ranks to identify the epitopes that will represent the group.

1 - Press Enter to review the methodology used to define the representative epitopes for the groups;
2 - Press Enter to review the representative epitopes. Then, select the option to view the list in .csv format and press Enter;
3 - Press Enter to proceed to the next step.
🖼️ Tutorial Image

Step 10: Search for variant sequences of the organism

1 - Press Enter to review the methodology for searching for variant sequences of the organism; 2 - Select the type of comparison you wish to perform between the predicted epitopes and pathogen variants (enter 1 for intraspecific or 2 for interspecific), then press Enter;

3 - Enter the number corresponding to the group you wish to compare and press Enter; 4 - Enter the host name and press Enter. This step is optional; if you do not wish to specify the host, simply press Enter;
5 - Enter the number corresponding to the type of variants for comparison (enter 1 for genotypes or 2 for the complete list) and press Enter to view the table of epitopes that have been conserved across variants; TIP! If no protein variants are found (this may occur because the Uniprot sequence database is more limited), you can manually enter one or more sequences in FASTA format in the next step to compare with the epitopes.
6 - Press Enter to proceed to the next step of the conservation analysis.
🖼️ Tutorial Image

Step 11: Conservation Across Variants

In this step, the epitopes will be compared with the variant sequences of the selected protein. From this step onward, our TheraEpiFlow tool will no longer use filters to limit the number of epitopes, leaving it up to the user to decide what to do with the generated data on conservation, population coverage, and epitope prediction for murines.

1 - Press Enter to review the epitope conservation methodology;
2 - Press Enter, type the option “y,” and press Enter if you want to paste one or more variant sequences. If not, type “N” and press Enter. Next, enter the number corresponding to the conservation percentage (e.g., if you want the epitope to have 100% (= 1.00 of the sequence matching across the analyzed variants), enter 1) and then press Enter;
3 - If you want to view the complete list of epitopes, type 1 and press Enter. Then press Enter again to proceed to the next step of population coverage.
🖼️ Tutorial Image
🖼️ Tutorial Image

Step 12: Population Coverage

In this step, the population coverage percentage for each epitope will be displayed, based on the frequency of HLA alleles present in the population.

1 - Press Enter to review the population coverage methodology, and then press Enter again to set the coverage level you would like to evaluate;
2 - If you want to set a cutoff percentage for population coverage, type 2 and press Enter. If you do not want to set a cutoff, skip this step and type 1 and press Enter to run the population coverage analysis;
3 - To view the complete table, type 1 and press Enter, then press Enter again to proceed to the next step.
🖼️ Tutorial Image
🖼️ Tutorial Image

Step 13: Murine epitope prediction

1 - Press Enter to view the murine epitope prediction methodology;

2 - Press Enter to select the murine MHC-I class for which you want to perform the prediction. Then, enter the number corresponding to the category of murine MHC-I and press Enter;
3 - After completing the epitope prediction for murines, enter 1 and press Enter to view the epitope table;
4 - Note the percentile values for this murine epitope prediction. This follows the same principle as epitope prediction for humans: the lower the value, the higher the binding affinity between the epitope and the HLA (binding affinity: high < 0.5, intermediate < 2.0, and low > 2.0).
🖼️ Tutorial Image
🖼️ Tutorial Image

Step 14: Adding murine epitope prediction data to the epitopes predicted for humans

1 - Press Enter to review the methodology used to combine the murine epitope prediction data with the epitope data predicted and analyzed through step 12;

2 - Press Enter to perform this data grouping, then type 1 and press Enter to view the final table.
🖼️ Tutorial Image

Step 15: Data Integration

In this final step, all the data collected so far is compiled into an HTML file, which you can download and open in your browser. This is a kind of “epitope calculator” that allows the user to assemble the synthetic antigen sequence in the order of epitopes, linkers, and adjuvants they desire, according to their needs.

1 - Press Enter to review the data integration methodology and the names of the files generated with the epitope tables and the “epitope calculator”; 2 - Press Enter, type “y”, and press Enter again to indicate whether you want to view the data in a columnar format; 3 - Type 2 to view the complete table, then press Enter;
4 - To generate the HTML file for the epitope calculator, press Enter twice; 5 - To save all files into a single .tar.gz file, press Enter twice; 6 - To complete all steps, press Enter twice;
7 -To access where these files generated throughout all stages are stored, navigate to your computer’s user directory by clicking “Linux,” then click the “Ubuntu” folder, and then click the “home” folder where you’ll find the folder with your username. In that folder, click on the folder for our tool named “TheraEpiFlow.” To access your project files, click on the “projects” folder; inside it, you’ll find the folder for the project you created. All projects created by the user will be stored in this folder, so it’s important that each project has a different name to make identification easier; Tip! If you want to access the tables generated after each step, go to the following folders: data>input: tables with initial data data > intermediate: folders for each step with their respective data tables data > output: tables with final data for the predicted and analyzed epitopes 8 - To access the epitope calculator, navigate through the folders in the sequence below until you reach the file REPORT_e7_hpv.html. In this calculator, you will have access to information about each epitope, and by clicking the + next to each epitope, you can assemble the sequence of your multiepitope antigen in the order you desire. You can also edit the linker and the position of the histidine tag in the sequence (C-terminal or N-terminal). Finally, you can explore our calculator by clicking on various parts. Folder path to access the calculator: Linux > Ubuntu > home > folder username > TheraEpiFlow > projects > e7_hpv > data > output > REPORT_e7_hpv.html
🖼️ Tutorial Image
🖼️ Tutorial Image
🖼️ Tutorial Image

Contact Information

For questions, suggestions, or technical support: 📧 Email: davi.ribeiro@ufpe.br 🏛️ Address: Laboratory of Molecular Studies and Experimental Therapy - LEMTE Department of Genetics Federal University of Pernambuco Recife, Pernambuco Brazil ⏱️ Response time: We typically respond within 24-48 hours during business days. 💬 For urgent matters, please include 'URGENT' in your email subject line.

How to Cite

If you use TheraEpiFlow in your research, please cite: Ribeiro, D. E., Invenção, M. C. V., Sena, M. G. A, et al. (2026). TheraEpiFlow: A The platform integrates multiple computational tools to facilitate the identification of potential vaccine candidates and immunotherapeutic targets. DOI: XX.XXXX/XXXXXXXX Additionally, please cite the individual tools used in the analysis: - NetMHCpan 4.1: [Citation] - MHCflurry 2.0: [Citation] - IEDB tools: [Citation]
